Last year, we asked Chattanoogans to invest in the essential services that impact their lives: police, fire, paving, affordable housing, and technology to address traffic congestion. This year's budget is about providing a return on that investment.  The approved balanced budget hold's our property tax steady in the face of inflation without cutting services due to a citywide initiative to identify cost savings and hold the line on expenses. The tax rate remains lower than the rate residents pay in Knoxville, Nashville and Memphis. Chattanooga’s tax burden per resident is also lower than Nashville and Knoxville. Swipe to see major investments within the FY27 budget, which begins on July 1.

Today’s Family Justice Center Strategic Summit reminded me once again how powerful a community can be when we come together with a shared purpose. We left the summit with meaningful progress and a stronger vision for promoting safety, healing, and justice for victims of domestic violence, sexual assault, child abuse, elder abuse, and human trafficking. Together, we explored ways to strengthen our coordinated response for adults and children, improve law enforcement partnerships, enhance access to Orders of Protection, engage faith communities, gather meaningful baseline data, expand healthcare and mental health services, strengthen governance, and increase community outreach and engagement. We built on the work from our planning session with Casey Gwinn in March. Casey motivated us and shined the light on best practices l.  Most importantly, every conversation centered on listening to and elevating the voices of survivors. Their experiences, courage, and insights must continue to guide our work. I am incredibly grateful for the many community leaders, professionals, advocates, and partners who invested their time and expertise today. A special thank you to Judge Christie Sell and Councilwoman Jenny Berz for their leadership and commitment to building a safer community for all. The solutions to violence require all of us. If you are interested in being part of this community effort and helping create a coordinated response that supports survivors and their families, we invite you to reach out to the Family Justice Center. Together, we can make a difference. #FamilyJusticeCenter #CommunityCollaboration #SurvivorCentered #EndDomesticViolence #EndSexualAssault #HopeAndHealing #Chattanooga #StrongerTogether

content_text: Join the City of Chattanooga Team Chattanooga , a vibrant and growing community of over 190,000 residents within a metropolitan area of nearly 600,000 is known as the "Scenic City" for a reason. Nestled along the Tennessee River and surrounded by the foothills of the Appalachian Mountains, we are a hub of outdoor adventure and innovation. Home to the University of Tennessee at Chattanooga (UTC) and Chattanooga State Community College , our city blends a rich industrial history with a tech-forward future—famously powered by the nation’s first community-wide "Gig" fiber-optic network.​ The City of Chattanooga was also recently designated as a "National Park City,” the first of its kind in the United States. Our Organization As a City, we are powered by a dedicated workforce of approximately ​ 2,600 employees committed to excellence in public service and making Chattanooga the best community in the country. Our team ensures our city thrives through a wide range of municipal services, including: Public Safety: Chattanooga Police and Fire Departments. Infrastructure Utilities: Public Works, Waste Resources, and specialized Stormwater management. Quality of Life: Award-winning Parks and Outdoors and Community Development systems and the Chattanooga Public Library. Transportation: The Chattanooga Metropolitan Airport (CHA) and citywide transit and pedestrian friendly initiatives. Innovation: Leading-edge smart-city programs with a commitment to cutting edge workforce development policies to ensure all our residents can get ahead. Why Choose Chattanooga? The City of Chattanooga’s workforce is the heartbeat of our community. We don't just offer jobs; we offer careers with purpose: making Chattanooga the best city in America. We are committed to being the premier employer of choice in Southeast Tennessee by providing: Competitive Compensation: Robust pay scales that reflect our commitment to our staff. Work-Life Balance: Generous paid time off and flexible scheduling where possible. Exceptional Wellness: Comprehensive health benefits and wellness programs designed for you and your family that deliver savings to you beyond your paycheck, featuring a centralized health clinic and pharmacy for all City employees. . Growth: A focus on professional development and upward mobility within the organization. ​The Great Outdoors: Your Backyard Playground: Chattanooga isn't just near nature, it's immersed in it. As one of the most biodiverse cities in North America, the "Scenic City" offers an ecological richness that few places on earth can claim. Whether you are an adrenaline junkie or a weekend wanderer, world-class adventure is always less than an hour away. ​Vertical Adventures: Scale legendary sandstone cliffs in what is widely considered the premier rock climbing hub of the Southeast. Water Rapids: From the high-octane whitewater kayaking of the Ocoee River to world-class bass fishing and peaceful paddling on the Tennessee River. Trails for Miles: Explore a massive network of mountain biking and hiking trails that wind through lush ridges, hidden waterfalls, and deep river gorges. In Chattanooga, "work-life balance" means you can finish your shift and be at a trailhead or on the water before the sun sets. Our mission is to work together to build a safe, vibrant, and sustainable city for all Chattanoogans. To do that, it takes a team of skilled and passionate individuals. So, if you’d like to call Chattanooga home – or maybe you already do! – and want to further your career through meaningful work that makes a true impact where you live, we’d love to talk to you. Salary: $22.74- $27.47 per hour (GS.8) Work Hours: Monday - Friday, 7:00 A.M. - 3:30 P.M. (EST) *Hours may vary due to work assignments and projects.* Work Location: 4063 N Hawthorne St, Chattanooga, TN FLSA Status: This is a Non-Exempt position. Department: Public Works- Solid Waste CLASSIFICATION SUMMARY: Incumbents in this classification drive and operate commercial vehicles, tractors, and other equipment with specialized functions that involve the operation of mechanisms to complete tasks in addition to driving and maneuvering the vehicle in order to complete tasks for various solid waste collection, maintenance, forestry and construction projects. Equipment in this classification includes commercial larger vehicles requiring a higher level of skill to drive and maneuver through small residential roads and vehicles with mechanisms that are somewhat complex to operate, requiring more dexterity and finesse. Classification is reserved for those positions where the driving and operating of included equipment is the primary function of the job. SERIES LEVEL: Truck Driver is a stand-alone position. ESSENTIAL FUNCTIONS: ( The following duties ARE NOT intended to serve as a comprehensive list of all duties performed by all employees in this classification, only a representative summary of the primary duties and responsibilities. Incumbent(s) may not be required to perform all duties listed and may be required to perform additional, position-specific duties.) Operates various equipment and vehicles to complete tasks related to the maintenance, upkeep, installation and construction of city grounds, property, utilities, rights-of-way, and other infrastructure for Public Works; drives vehicles over public roads, worksites, right-of-way, parks, public works facilities, and other city property; maneuvers in and around traffic, parked cars, equipment, utilities, and other obstacles; obeys all traffic laws and safety guidelines, monitors immediate environment and work are to ensure the safety of the operator, other workers, the equipment and the public. Some positions may drive tandem and tri-axle dump trucks to transport and haul materials between original and final destinations for use in maintenance and construction projects; positions vehicle to allow for the most efficient and effective loading or dumping of materials; secures all doors, gates, and fasteners to prevent spillage or loss of load; operates dumping mechanism to dump materials at appropriate locations. Operates mowing tractors with articulated boom mowing system to mow rights-of-way and other City property along steep slopes, ditches, and hard-to-reach areas inaccessible to most mowing apparatuses; scouts path for and removes rocks, debris and other obstacles that may cause damage to the equipment, property or the public; determines proper settings and adjusts boom for the proper angle, height, and trajectory; maneuvers and monitors boom mower for optimal operation. Operate knuckle-boom truck to collect trash, limbs and other debris from work sites, parks, and other City property and transport it to the landfill; positions vehicle alongside targeted debris; operates truck- or trailer-mounted knuckle-boom arm to pick up debris and load it into the truck; secures all doors, gates, and fasteners to prevent spillage or loss of load during transport; dumps material at an appropriate location. Operates and maneuvers knuckle-boom and articulated arm to pick up limbs; maintains a constant awareness of the environment to ensure the safety of the operator, other crew workers, and the public. Operates street sweepers along a prescribed route to remove dirt and debris from City streets and parking lots; maneuvers in and out of traffic and around parked cars, taking care to reduce congestion and maintain safety; determines proper settings and adjusts brushes for proper angle and height, maintaining proper curb distance for optimum coverage. Drives low-boy and other tractor-trailer rigs to transport heavy construction equipment and materials to and from work-sites; maneuvers equipment to load and unload from the trailer, operating mechanisms as necessary to position for safe transportation. Drives a variety of garbage trucks along a prescribed commercial or residential route to collect refuse; makes frequent stops at appropriate intervals to position truck near refuse containers; retrieves refuse container and attaches to automated dumping mechanism; dumps refuse and returns container; maintains the pace of collection activities to ensure timely completion of the assigned route. Drives fuel truck to transport fuel to equipment at work sites; operates the mechanism to fuel equipment as necessary; applies knowledge of hazardous materials when transporting, handling, and responding to spills involving gasoline, diesel fuel, and other flammable/volatile liquids. Drives bucket and/or lift truck to work site and operates lift mechanisms to elevate crews performing various maintenance, upkeep and installation for traffic systems, tree trimming and other tasks requiring access to high places; positions vehicle and lift-platform to ensure optimum access to the required location and avoid power lines, low branches, traffic and other hazards; monitors activities to ensure the safety of crew workers. Operates a jet-flushing/vacuum/viewer truck for the purpose of cleaning out storm drain and sanitary sewer pipe, manholes, catch basins and other brick and concrete drainage structures in order to maintain the flow throughout the stormwater drainage and sewer waste systems. Drives trucks with a side-mounted articulated vacuum boom along an assigned route for the purpose of picking up leaves, litter, and other yard waste from rights-of-way and out of drainage measurements in order to maintain stormwater drainage flow throughout the entire system. Inspects and prepares equipment for safe and proper operation prior to beginning work for the day; performs light maintenance on equipment which includes refueling and refilling lubricants; cleans vehicles, beds, and mounted equipment upon completion of assignments; secures vehicle after use. Sanitize and clean assigned equipment daily. Performs basic and semi-skilled labor as appropriate to assist in the completion of maintenance, upkeep and construction projects; picks up paper, limbs and other debris; uses hand tools and operates motorized tools as necessary to complete the assigned task. May remove dead animals and trees from the right of way as well as public roads. May answer 911 emergency calls for oil spills as well as black ice. May assist in contacting departments or personnel for an emergency situation. May be required to use, carry and answer cell phones, other devices, and/or two-way radio as determined by their job duties and their supervisor. Must meet regular attendance requirements. Must be able to maintain good interpersonal relationships with staff, co-workers, managers and citizens. Must accomplish the essential functions of the job, with or without reasonable accommodations, in a timely manner. May be assigned or reassigned to any division within the department. Performs other related duties as required. This position is deemed essential during inclement weather situations, and must report to or remain at work, even when administrative closings are announced, as determined by the Department Head. DEPARTMENT SPECIFIC DUTIES (if any): When working in Forestry division: Uses of chainsaws, rigging lines, pulleys, and cranes. Working in high traffic areas with the use of signs, cones, and flaggers and in the vicinity of power lines, moving traffic or directly above buildings. Removes and cleans up dead or hazardous trees. Available on call for storm work or downed trees. May train personnel. MINIMUM QUALIFICATIONS: High School Diploma or GED and one (1) year of any combination of relevant education, experience, and/or training sufficient to successfully perform the essential functions of this job; or any combination of equivalent experience and education. LICENSING AND CERTIFICATIONS: Valid Class A, Class A restricted or Class B commercial driver license. Requires appropriate license endorsement(s) related to the area of assignment. KNOWLEDGE SKILLS: Knowledge of occupational hazards and safety precautions; general equipment and maintenance principles; materials and equipment utilized in assigned area of responsibility; demonstrated ability to drive and operate assigned equipment; operation of commercial vehicles that consist of self-contained specialized equipment. Typical equipment operated might include, but is not limited to: tractor-trailers, low boys, fuel trucks, bucket trucks, mechanical street sweepers, knuckle-boom trucks, articulated boom mowers, oil distributors, jet-flushing/vacuum trucks, CCTV equipped trucks and automated garbage trucks with an articulated arm. Must be able to use electronic devices to receive and track work. Position requires minimal, but daily public interaction and must be customer service centered, focused, and able to recognize or resolve customer needs according to departmental practices. PHYSICAL DEMANDS: Positions in this class typically require climbing, balancing, stooping, kneeling, crouching, crawling, reaching, standing, walking, pushing, pulling, lifting, fingering, grasping, feeling, talking, hearing, seeing and repetitive motions. WORK ENVIRONMENT: Medium Work: Exerting up-to 50 pounds of force occasionally, and/or up-to 20 pounds of force frequently, and/or up-to 10 pounds of force constantly to move objects. SPECIAL REQUIREMENTS: Safety Sensitive: Y Department of Transportation - CDL: Y Child Sensitive: N SUPPLEMENTAL INFORMATION: This position requires pre-employment screening(s) that include: background check / drug screening / lift test The City of Chattanooga, Tennessee is an Equal Opportunity Employer. In compliance with the Americans with Disabilities Act, the City will provide reasonable accommodations to qualified individuals with disabilities and encourage both prospective and current employees to discuss potential accommodations with the employer. How big are the new wastewater storage tanks? Come with us as we take an exclusive look inside…  Checking back in on the e2i2 Equalization Station off of I-75, the equalization tanks are almost ready for use. The West Chickamauga EQ Site features two 15 million gallon tanks for storing excess flow in the sanitary sewer system during heavy rainfall events. This additional storage capacity will prevent sanitary sewer overflows (SSOs) into South Chickamauga Creek and the Tennessee River.  Thank you to the Chattanooga Wastewater Department for the hard work toward this public safety initiative!

Department: Community Development CLASSIFICATION SUMMARY: Incumbents in this classification are responsible for cleaning and maintaining City facilities, buildings, and landscapes. The role assesses and anticipates maintenance needs, communicating needs proactively with direct supervisor. Work is performed under close supervision (although most tasks are routine with little supervision required). SERIES LEVEL: This is a stand-alone position. Performs various duties in the cleaning and maintenance of various City facilities, buildings and landscapes including cleaning restrooms, sweeping, mopping, cleaning various types of flooring including terrazzo, tile, rubber and cork, vacuuming, carpet cleaning, operating specialized cleaning equipment, washing windows, removing trash and litter. May perform minor repairs and maintenance such as replacing light fixtures or unclogging pipes. Assists in cleaning and sanitizing restrooms and break areas. Performs assorted custodial duties such as sweeping sidewalks and streets, and collecting/picking up brush, garbage and recycling materials. Maintains a neat and orderly supply room; ensures cleaning and maintenance supplies are stocked. Washes windows. Report any safety, sanitary, or fire hazards to the direct supervisor. Identifies building cleaning issues, recommends solutions, and implements processes to improve and streamline processes. Keeps buildings secure by locking doors. Ensures work environments are adequate and safe. Must meet regular attendance requirements. Must be able to maintain good interpersonal relationships with staff, co-workers, managers and citizens. Please share to help spread the word!A #TNSilverAlert has been issued on behalf of the Chattanooga Police Department for 83-year-old Betty Miller.  Betty was last seen on Monday leaving her home in Chattanooga and is believed to be traveling in a 2008 Red Chevy Cobalt with TN tag PD69088. Betty is 5’4” tall, 165 lbs., with gray hair and brown eyes. She has a medical condition that may impair her ability to return safely without assistance.  If you have seen Betty please call the Chattanooga Police Department at 423-698-2525 or TBI at 1-800-TBI-FIND.

No, we’re not building an underground water park. 😎 Today we got a look inside the tanks being built along I-75 near our Brown Acres Golf Course  These giant tanks along I-75 are actually part of a major project that will store up to 30 million gallons of stormwater during heavy rains. Think of them as Chattanooga’s version of a giant “pause button” for excess water and help clean.  They may look like something out of a sci-fi movie now, but by 2027 they’ll help protect our water system and keep local waterways cleaner like the South Chickamauga Creek. Now, this project will not be complete till fall of 2027, which means the greenway from Camp Jordan to Brained Levee will remain closed till then.  Also, if anyone asks, yes… we checked. Echoes are incredible in there. 🎤😂l

Chattanooga was blasted by storms Monday night (6/22/26), making things very busy for Red Shift firefighters across the city. Calls quickly poured into 911, like wires down, fires, flooding, and large trees on homes. We have no injuries to report at this time, thankfully. The structural damage caused by downed trees displaced several families. Trees fell in multiple locations, including:  4100 block of Kirkland Avenue 300 block of S Howell Avenue 800 block of Mount Vernon Circle  2000 block of N Chamberlain Avenue 3300 block of Adkins Road  Please do not drive through flooded roadways or drive over downed power lines.

Brainerd Hills, your new pavilion at Benham-Williams Park is officially open!  Huge kudos to everyone who worked to pull this off! This is the first "in-house" pavilion completely designed and constructed by our world-class team. Thank you to District 6 Councilwoman Jenni Berz and the Brainerd Hills Neighborhood Association for rallying the neighborhood for a rainy-day ribbon cutting and cookout in the park! It was a great chance to put this structure to the test, and it passed with flying colors. We are looking forward to opening more great structures like this in parks across our beautiful city.

Let's send another shout out to civil engineer and project manager, Elizabeth Goss, (holding paper) for letting us tag along and visit the equalization tanks near Brown Acres Golf course.  Both tanks are 240 feet in diameter and provide storage capacity of up to 15 million gallons each of excess wastewater during significant rain events.  This structure diverts excess wastewater, not flood water,  from existing buried sewer system pipeline when excess wastewater otherwise overflows into local waterways.   #CleanWaterMatters #waterquality #cleanwater #PublicWorks #engineers #civilengineering #womeninpublicworks #womenengineersrock

A ribbon was cut today… but the real win happens underground. ✂️💧 The new Kanasita Equalization Basin and Pump Station Project can store up to 5 million gallons of excess wastewater during heavy rain events, helping reduce sanitary sewer overflows and protect our neighborhoods, waterways, and community. And that’s not all—this project also replaced aging infrastructure and addressed long-standing odor concerns for nearby residents. Not every superhero wears a cape. Sometimes it’s a 5-million-gallon tank quietly doing its job beneath the surface. 😉 Today, we celebrate another major milestone for Clear Chattanooga and a cleaner, more resilient future for our city. 📸 Drone photo courtesy of Cool New Media. #ClearChattanooga #Chattanooga #Infrastructure #CommunityFirst #WastewaterWorks

We hear your concerns about the odors, and we want to show you exactly what’s happening behind the scenes at the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus. Upgrading an aging wastewater system is a massive, highly complex process. In the video below, you can get a firsthand look at the major infrastructure upgrades we are currently undertaking to protect our waterways and improve our community: 💡Massive Infrastructure: We manage over 1,200 miles of underground piping and 80 pump stations to process 65 million gallons of wastewater from the surrounding region every single day. 💡Preventing Overflows: To protect the Tennessee River during heavy rains, we are utilizing 30 million gallons of new storage capacity. In fact, the clean water we put back into the river is actually cleaner than the river itself! 💡Odor Control: We know the smell is an issue we need to tackle. We currently utilize five different odor control systems to manage the greenhouse gases emissions from daily sewage, as well as the 80 different local industries that discharge into our system.  💡Green Energy Innovation: We recently announced a new initiative to convert wastewater methane directly into electricity. This will eventually take our campus off the electric grid and save up to $300,000 a month in energy costs. These upgrades take time, but our staff is working hard every day to move our city’s systems forward. Want to better understand the science behind it all? Tap the link below or in our bio for a simple breakdown of exactly how our wastewater treatment process works:👇 https://www.local3news.com/…/article_13c310a8-d534-479f-97c…

We hear you, Chattanooga! 🗣️ It’s no secret that facility upgrades have been a long time coming, and our team is hard at work making them a reality. We are proud to be working alongside Mayor Tim Kelly to implement these crucial improvements at the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus. Thank you to our community for your patience as we build a cleaner, fresher future for our city! Read more about the timeline and what’s underway below: 👇💧WHAT'S THAT SMELL? For years, Chattanooga residents have occasionally noticed unpleasant sewage odors drifting across parts of the city. Mayor Tim Kelly says significant improvements underway at the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus should help reduce those smells in the coming years.⬇️

Wrapping up an incredible final day at the Tennessee Environmental Network Show of the South Conference! 🌿 A massive thank you to our speakers — Bill Cannon, Jacob McCrary, Travis Jones, and David Taylor — for representing the evolution happening within the Wastewater Department. We aren’t just a “sewer plant” anymore. We are stepping into our true identity as the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus. We’re shifting from stigma to stewardship, from waste to raw materials, and from consumption to circularity. That shift is exactly why we took home the 2025 Environmental Stewardship Award for Water Quality! 🏆 But we’re equally proud of what’s happening internally. From crushing 6,000+ work orders a year to launching our new employee-focused “Operation Career Ladder” this July, we are turning everyday labor into lifelong, empowered careers. Protecting our environment, empowering our people. Water to water. Earth to earth. 🌍💧

Thank you, Mayor Kelly, for recognizing Jacob's hard work! The Career Ladder program has been a game-changer for our staff, ensuring they have the tools to grow and succeed right here in Chattanooga. We're incredibly proud of Jacob and this well-deserved Mayoral Award! 👏 If anyone is interested in joining a team that prioritizes professional growth, we’d love to have you apply for our Director of Collection System role! You can find the details and apply here: https://fa-eqto-saasfaprod1.fa.ocs.oraclecloud.com/…/job/25…Thanks to Jacob McCrary — director of operations at the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus — when you work in our Wastewater Department you can grow your career through training and testing. Over the last several years, Jacob has been refining his Career Ladder program to ensure all employees in the department can progress and prepare for leadership roles. I was honored to present him with the Mayoral Award in April.  Join this incredible team — CHA Wastewater Department is currently hiring for a Director of Collection System.

Exciting things happening in Chatt 🔥🔥We’re here to answer the question you’ve been asking — what are those gigantic structures popping up along I-75? In a way to combat sanitary sewer overflows, our Wastewater Department has been hard at work with its partners to construct e2i2 Equalization Stations. During a storm event, these will: Send wastewater from the sewer lines to the diversion structure, that then funnels to a 48-inch gravity line (that’s about 20-ft deep) toward the pump storage. The pump storage will then send it to one of the two 15 million-gallon tanks (made of steel and shotcrete). This process will be powered by an electrical house with an 800kw generator and there will also be odor control ventilation for the pump station and tanks. Once the storm event is over, our Wastewater team will send the water downstream through the gravity line that connects to the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus where it will get treated before being sent back to the Tennessee River. Construction began in 2025 and is on track for its 2027 completion. So far, the project has also generated $39 million in local investment and supporting jobs in the region.

Murray Hills Drive temporarily closed for sewer repairs Road expected to reopen March 27, weather permitting CHATTANOOGA, Tenn. (March 5, 2026) — Murray Hills Drive, located off Highway 58, is temporarily closed while crews with the City of Chattanooga Wastewater Department complete sewer repairs. The closure is necessary to repair underground sewer infrastructure and help prevent potential service disruptions. Contractors will perform excavations and point repairs to underground sewer lines through March 18. Once underground work is completed, crews will begin asphalt repairs and concrete restoration, which are expected to be finished by March 25. The roadway is expected to reopen March 27, 2026, after the concrete and asphalt have properly cured. Drivers should follow the approved detour route during the closure and allow additional travel time while work is underway. The construction schedule is weather dependent and subject to change. See the attached detour map below for the approved traffic route.

We're back on Facebook and excited to reconnect with our community! 👋 Did you know the Chattanooga Wastewater Department became an independent department in 2022? We are proud to serve you directly. One of our mottos? "We Take Your Crap Seriously" 💩 Sure, it gets a laugh—but behind the humor is a team working 24/7 to protect public health and the Tennessee River. (Fun fact: Our river system is widely considered the most biodiverse in North America—home to more fish species than all of Europe combined! 🐟). What does it actually take to keep Chattanooga flowing? Here is the breakdown: 🚛 COLLECT: We maintain 1,263 miles of sewer infrastructure (that’s the distance from Chattanooga to Boston!). 🧪 TREAT: We treat 230 million gallons per day at the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus (enough to fill 350 Olympic pools). ✅ TRUTH: We don't just flush it. We treat every drop before it returns to the river. Save this post for reference! 📌 • Call 311: For sewer backups, leaky manholes, or billing questions. • Call (423) 643-7400: For sewer taps, location services, or new industry permits. Thanks for following along. We're here to serve!

Local historian, Sam Hall, has shared digitized images of the Walnut Street Bridge, sparking discussions about the cost of repairs. However, do stakeholders fully comprehend the intricacies of renovating a structure dating back to the late 19th century? Many assume a fresh coat of paint and replacement of old surface planks would suffice. In reality, the bridge requires extensive structural remediation. What does this entail? Essentially, it means that simply installing a new walkway on a deteriorating foundation is not a viable solution. This massive structure necessitates a thorough examination, and upon peeling away layers of repair, additional areas requiring attention are likely to be uncovered.

The final type of scam we're highlighting is law enforcement scams. In these cases, fraudsters will contact you while impersonating an officer, Federal agent, or bondsman. They'll claim you have an unpaid ticket, missed jury duty, have an unresolved warrant, or that you need to pay bond for a relative in jail, and then try to get you to pay them using gift cards, cryptocurrency, or digital payment services (Zelle, Apple Cash, PayPal, etc.). To be more believable, these scammers may even spoof a real law enforcement agency's phone number, tricking your caller ID. Here's what you need to know: 1. No legitimate law enforcement agency will ask you to pay fees, balances, or bonds with these payment methods. 1. Today's Chattanooga Times Free Press includes a column, "Times Opinion: Chattanooga’s proposed budget has the wrong priorities."  While an opinion piece, facts and context matter. The following was sent to the paper and posted here to provide that information for all who wish to read about CPD's role in immigration enforcement...a topic CPD's Chief of Police has repeatedly publicly explained. Clarifying Mischaracterizations of CPD’s Role in Immigration Enforcement Thank you once again, Ben Sessoms, for providing the Chattanooga Police Department an opportunity to speak to our community about how its officers enforce laws regarding immigration and fill in the context that your column continues to omit. The section of Sessoms’ recent opinion column on the city’s budget that includes a section about immigration enforcement is factually misleading and reflects a misunderstanding of how local law enforcement operates. The assertion that CPD is engaged in “carrying out President Donald Trump’s ongoing mass deportation crackdown locally” mischaracterizes both federal and local roles and confuses the distinct systems of law enforcement that are in place in our city, state, and country.  This is especially important given Sessoms’ claim that “Of the over 500 immigrants transferred to federal immigration custody from the Hamilton County Jail in 2025, a majority of the initial arrests were made by the Chattanooga Police Department.” That statement requires context that his column does not provide. Transfers to federal immigration custody from the jail do not indicate that CPD is engaged in immigration enforcement or selective targeting based on immigration status. Rather, these transfers typically occur through standard criminal justice procedures following arrests for violations of state or local law.  It is important for community members, including local journalists, to understand that local police enforce state and local criminal laws, not federal civil immigration law. Immigration enforcement is the responsibility of federal authorities.  While Sessoms is certainly entitled and even paid to express an opinion, even opinion writers have an obligation to present complete and accurate facts to support their arguments. In this case, he could have easily referenced the broader coverage in the very paper that employs him for the additional context. Specifically, Siena Duncan’s reporting on CPD Chief John Chambers’ presentation to City Council earlier this year explaining in detail how the CPD is, in fact, not acting as an immigration enforcement agency but is operating within established law. Duncan’s reporting also notes that total arrests of Hispanic people by CPD declined 7% in 2025, and arrests of Hispanic people specifically for driving without a license declined 11%.  Again, thank you, Ben and Times Free Press, for the opportunity to present facts to your readers and our community. The most important takeaway from this is simple and straightforward: the Chattanooga Police Department does not make the law, officers enforce the law as written and do not target individuals based on where they come from, what they look like, or the language they speak. Elisa Myzal Director, Public Affairs Chattanooga Police Department
